query: exon 38 and 39 KMT2D missense variants and Kabuki syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Genetic Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic testing can be approached in 2 ways. When phenotypic findings suggest a diagnosis of Kabuki syndrome, genetic testing can include either single-gene testing or a multigene panel. Single gene testing with sequence analysis will detect missense, nonsense, splice site variants, and intragenic insertions/deletions. [5] Some specialists recommended that sequence analysis of KMT2D be performed first. If no pathogenic variants are found, performing gene-targeted deletion/duplication analysis is recommended. If no pathogenic variant is found, both sequence analysis and gene-targeted deletion/duplication analysis should be performed on KDM6A . Alternatively, a multigene panel that includes KMT2D , KDM6A , and other genes of interest can help identify a pathogenic variant while limiting variants of uncertain significance and pathogenic variants not explained by the underlying phenotype. In patients with KMT2D pathogenic variants, >99% were detectable by sequence analysis. In patients found to have KDM6A pathogenic variants, approximately 80% were detected with sequence analysis, and approximately 20% were detected with gene-targeted deletion/duplication analysis. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The KMT2D mutation affects normal growth and development by interrupting histone methylation related to gene expression. This mutated gene was the first pathogenic gene recognized in Kabuki syndrome, also known as MLL2 . Recognized in 2010, this is the most common gene mutation in patients with Kabuki syndrome, found in 75% of cases. [1] [4] This condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. [3] The proportion of cases caused by a de novo KMT2D mutation is unknown but is likely high. [5] The KMT2D gene is located on chromosome 12q13, which is expressed in most cells in the body. This encodes a large nucleoprotein that regulates histone methyltransferase activity, which methylates the Lys4 residue of histone H3 (H3K4). H3K4Me (methylated H3K4) activates transcriptional initiation sites on up to 75% of human genes. [1] More than 50 mutations at different sites of KMT2D have been found, including nonsense, missense, frameshift mutations, small deletions, and splice site variants. [1] [3] Rare mosaic KMT2D cases have been identified. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The KDM6A mutation affects normal growth and development through similar mechanisms of KMT2D , as both genes belong to the same multiprotein complex. KDM6A (also known as UTX ) is the second most pathologic gene, found in less than 5% of patients with Kabuki syndrome. [1] This mutation is inherited in an X-linked manner; the gene is located on Xp11.23 and encodes a histone (H3)-lysine 27-specific demethylase that interacts with KMT2D in regulating gene expression in the activating signal cointegrator-2-containing complex (ASCOM). [1] [3] [5] Mutations of KDM6A include nonsense, frameshift, splice site, missense, large deletions, small insertions, and small deletions; a rare mosaic case of KDM6A has previously been identified. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic testing : Confirm the diagnosis by performing genetic testing, focusing on mutations in the KMT2D or KDM6A genes.

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: KSK is caused by variants in the KMT2D and KDM6A genes. Seventy-five percent of cases are secondary to either de novo or inherited pathogenic/likely pathogenic variants in KMT2D . In comparison, 5% of the cases are attributed to variants in KDM6A , which has an X-linked dominant inheritance pattern; the etiology for the remaining 20% of cases remains unknown. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Genetic Testing
retrieved_docs.content: When phenotypic findings do not suggest a diagnosis of KS, comprehensive genomic testing should be performed with either exome sequencing or genome sequencing. Disorder-specific genome-wide changes in deoxyribonucleic acid methylation profiles are found in peripheral blood leukocytes in patients with KS This approach to diagnosis can be considered in patients who have phenotypic findings suggestive of Kabuki syndrome but in whom no pathogenic variant has been found in KMT2D or KDM6A or in patients with phenotypic findings suggestive of KS who have had a KMT2D or KDM6A variant of uncertain significance identified. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The penetrance of pathogenic variants in KMT2D is not complete. The penetrance is unknown for pathogenic variants of KDM6A . Given the variable expressivity, patients with a mild phenotype may remain undiagnosed. [5] Genetic defects remain unknown in about 20% of patients with KS. [1] [4] Patients without KMT2D or KDM6A mutations are often referred to as having Kabuki-like syndrome. Proband mutations suggestive of Kabuki-like syndrome are found in the HNRNPK gene. Reported copy number variations associated with Kabuki-like syndrome include MACROD2 gene microdeletion and 8p22-p23.1 duplication. Mutations in KDM1A , RAP1A , and RAP1B have also been implicated; other genes associated with a Kabuki-like phenotype likely exist. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Families should receive genetic counseling on the inheritance pattern of Kabuki syndrome, such as the autosomal dominant pattern for KMT2D versus X-linked inheritance for KDM6A , which is essential for decision-making for subsequent pregnancies and existing siblings of the patients with this condition. Genetic counseling is also necessary for patients for future family planning. Parents should be counseled that in the rare case that a parent has an affected proband, the risk of a sibling having KS is 50%. For a child with a KDM6A mutation, parents should be counseled about X-linked inheritance; if the mother has an affected proband, the chance of passing it on to another child is 50% in each pregnancy. Parents should be counseled that boys who inherit the mutation will be affected and that girls who inherit the mutation will be heterozygous—and may or may not have features of KS. Patients and families should be counseled that once a causative mutation is identified, prenatal testing for this syndrome is available. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Cognitive
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with KS often have hypophrenia, presenting with varying degrees of language and optomotor deficits. The most common deficit is dysarthria, characterized by poor sound quality, hypernasality, tone distortion, and inaccurate consonants. Most patients demonstrate a decline in the ability to understand and express language. Verbal Comprehension Index (VCI) and Full Scale Intellectual Quotient (FSIQ) scores of patients with KMT2D mutations were found to be 10 points lower when compared to other mutation types. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Cognitive
retrieved_docs.content: Mild to moderate intellectual disability affects most patients with Kabuki syndrome. [5] This is partly due to a deficiency in postnatal neurogenesis as a direct result of the KMT2D mutation, suggesting a neural basis for cognitive and intellectual impairment. [4] Average intelligence quotient (IQ) scores with KMT2D pathogenic variants have been reported to be in the high 50s to high 60s, although individuals with a more severe disability may be nonverbal. Severe intellectual disabilities may be found in patients with truncating variants in the first half of KMT2D or those with a whole deletion of KMT2D . Rare pathogenic variants of KMT2D and KDM6A have reported IQ levels above 70. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Differences in Phenotype
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple syndromes such as Wiedemann-Steiner syndrome, CHARGE syndrome, and Turner syndrome are thought to have some genotypic and phenotypic overlap with Kabuki syndrome. [1] There are also reported cases of patients with variants of KMT2D that meet genetic and clinical criteria for the diagnosis of Kabuki syndrome but who also have unusual features of KS suggestive of a new underlying disease mechanism. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity reviews the genetic basis for Kabuki syndrome, its clinical manifestations, diagnostic considerations, and management. By participating in this activity, healthcare professionals enhance their knowledge of diagnostic accuracy, improve patient care, and stay current with evolving practices when managing Kabuki syndrome. This course also highlights the importance of an interprofessional healthcare team in providing comprehensive, individualized care for patients with this rare syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Kabuki syndrome is a rare inherited genetic syndrome caused by mutations in either the KMT2D or KDM6A gene. These mutations affect methylation, leading to abnormal growth and development. Kabuki syndrome is often recognized by the characteristic facial features that are thought to resemble traditional Japanese Kabuki theatre actors. The condition has a heterogeneous phenotype and affects multiple body systems, leading to craniofacial abnormalities, hearing loss, vision problems, congenital heart defects, feeding difficulties, immune dysfunction, poor growth, skeletal and dermatoglyphic abnormalities, and difficulties with cognition and development. There is no treatment other than symptomatic management and prevention of complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathogenesis of Kabuki syndrome is defined by epigenetic modifications during embryogenesis that regulate the expression level of genes (mainly KMT2D and KDM6A ) by the on and off switch of chromatin. [1] KMT2D protein is a histone (H3) lysine methyltransferase protein, and KDM6A is a histone demethylase protein, both with complementary functions. KMT2D protein plays a role in opening the chromatin, while KDM6A acts by closing the chromatin. The switch between open and closed chromatin states affects the transcription process, ultimately causing the disease. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the characteristic facial features associated with Kabuki syndrome, including arched eyebrows with sparse lateral third, long palpebral fissures with eversion of the lateral third, prominent ears, and a broad, depressed nasal tip. Differentiate Kabuki syndrome from other genetic syndromes with similar clinical presentations (eg, Cornelia de Lange syndrome) by recognizing specific clinical features and obtaining genetic testing for mutations in the KMT2D or KDM6A genes. Apply supportive therapies and interventions to address specific symptoms and complications of Kabuki syndrome, such as speech therapy for communication difficulties, physical therapy for musculoskeletal issues, and early intervention programs for developmental delays. Collaborate with other members of the interprofessional healthcare team to develop and implement a comprehensive care plan tailored to the individual needs of patients with Kabuki syndrome, ensuring continuity of care and optimizing outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Kabuki syndrome is a challenging clinical diagnosis because many characteristics are absent in the initial neonatal period and may not become evident until later in life. Those with the KMT2D mutation are more likely to have clinical features characteristic of this syndrome in infancy compared to those with a KDM6A mutation. Multiple scoring systems have been developed to diagnose patients with KS who have not had genetic testing or who have had genetic testing with no identifiable etiology. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1439
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic disturbances that encompass specific conditions such as اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي and فرط البروتينات الشحمية. This category also includes the clinical concept ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) as one of its members, indicating a link between lipid abnormalities and early‑onset hyperglycemia. In classification systems, disorders of lipid metabolism are mapped to فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ, a form of familial hyperlipidemia. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that the trait is transmitted through a single copy of the altered gene from an affected parent. Together, these relationships highlight how lipid metabolism disorders are interconnected with other metabolic disorders, specific disease entities, and genetic inheritance mechanisms, informing both diagnostic categorization and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2669
retrieved_docs.content: Cataract, also referred to by its Arabic term إعتام عدسة العين الشيخوخي (senile lens opacity), is a condition characterized by clouding of the eye’s lens that leads to visual impairment. Krasnow Qazi syndrome is a genetic disorder in which cataract is a recognized clinical manifestation, contributing to the visual deficits observed in affected individuals. Identifying cataract as a feature of Krasnow Qazi syndrome aids clinicians in diagnosing the syndrome and planning appropriate ophthalmologic management to preserve vision.

The **fluid and electrolyte disorders** node is inversely related to **اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات**, maps to **ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما**, and classifies **حالات اضطراب توازن السائل والإلكتروليت والقاعدي الحمضي** (fluid‑electrolyte‑acid‑base imbalance). It is associated with the severe manifestation **غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري** and includes the electrolyte derangements **فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم** (hyperkalemia), **متلازمة نقصُ البوتاسيوم في الدَّم** (hypokalemia syndrome), **زيادة الفجوة الأنيونية**, **فرط حمض يوريك الدم** (hyperuricemia), **متلازمة نقص صوديوم الدم**

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 972
retrieved_docs.content: The category “الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ)” (congenital, familial and genetic disorders) encompasses several specific conditions, including “الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور” (diffuse encephalitis around the axis), “عدم تحمل بروتين اللايسين” (lysine‑protein intolerance), “فالج طفلي” (infantile cerebral palsy) and “متلازمة ستيكلر” (Stickler syndrome). This SMQ group is itself classified as a member of broader disease categories such as “other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions,” “genitourinary congenital anomalies,” and “sexually transmitted infections.” These hierarchical relationships illustrate how individual hereditary and degenerative nervous system disorders are organized within larger clinical groupings, underscoring the interconnected nature of genetic, congenital, and systemic conditions and the importance of integrated assessment and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1899
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is linked to pancreatic cancer, indicating that this genetic or clinical syndrome has a mapped association with the development of cancer of the pancreas. Recognizing this connection is important for clinicians, as patients diagnosed with Bard‑Pic syndrome may require heightened surveillance for pancreatic malignancy and tailored management strategies to address the potential increased risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1214
retrieved_docs.content: Autosomal dominant inheritance is the mode of transmission for فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (hyperlipidemia type IIa). This genetic pattern means that a single copy of the altered gene inherited from either parent is sufficient to cause the disorder. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is characterized by markedly elevated low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels, which increase the risk of premature atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Because the condition follows an autosomal dominant pattern, it often appears in successive generations, and first‑degree relatives of an affected individual have a 50 % chance of inheriting the same lipid abnormality. Recognizing the autosomal dominant inheritance of فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is essential for family screening, early diagnosis, and implementation of lipid‑lowering therapies to reduce cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic conditions that include hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) as a specific type of lipid disorder. Within this category, elevated blood glucose and new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)) are also considered members, highlighting the clinical overlap between dyslipidemia and early glucose dysregulation. The disorder group is mapped to familial hyperlipidemia type IIa (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ), a well‑characterized lipid abnormality that follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, as indicated by the relationship between Autosomal dominant inheritance and فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ. This interconnected framework underscores how lipid metabolism disorders, specific hyperlipoproteinemia subtypes, and early diabetic manifestations are linked both pathophysiologically and genetically, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: The Standardized MedDRA Query “الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ)” (congenital, familial and genetic disorders) aggregates a diverse set of disorders. It includes “other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions,” the metabolic disorder “عدم تحمل بروتين اللايسين” (lysine‑protein intolerance), the developmental condition “فالج طفلي” (infantile cerebral palsy), “متلازمة ستيكلر” (Stickler syndrome), and even “sexually transmitted infections.” This SMQ is itself listed as a member of the broader category “genitourinary congenital anomalies,” indicating that the genetic‑disorder query is encompassed within that larger congenital‑anomaly group. Within the SMQ, the specific neurological condition “الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور” (diffuse encephalitis around the axis) is both used by “other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions” and classified as part of the SMQ. Pharmacologically, the antiepileptic drug Valproat (valproate) is contraindicated for patients with diffuse encephalitis around the axis, highlighting an important therapeutic restriction for this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2198
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary disorder of nervous system is a broad category of genetic conditions affecting the nervous system, and شقيقة فالجية عائلية (familial hemiplegic migraine) is a specific instance within this category. شقيقة فالجية عائلية represents a hereditary form of migraine that is characterized by transient hemiplegia and other neurological symptoms, illustrating how a hereditary disorder of the nervous system can manifest as a distinct migraine subtype.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1763
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic conditions that include specific entities such as فرط البروتينات الشحمية, which is recognized as a particular type of lipid metabolism disorder. Within this same category, the condition ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) is listed as a member, indicating that early‑onset diabetes is considered part of the spectrum of lipid metabolism abnormalities. The SMQ is further characterized by the clinical effect تأثير إدرار البول, reflecting the polyuric manifestation commonly seen in new‑onset hyperglycemia. Additionally, the disorder فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is mapped to the disorders of lipid metabolism group and is known to follow an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, highlighting a genetic basis for this specific hyperlipidemic condition. Together, these relationships illustrate how lipid metabolism disorders can intersect with early diabetes presentations and hereditary factors, underscoring the importance of integrated assessment and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism encompass conditions such as فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia). Within this broader category, فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is a specific manifestation that is mapped to the class of lipid‑metabolism disorders. Type IIa hyperlipidemia follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that Autosomal dominant inheritance is the genetic transmission mechanism for this particular disorder. Recognizing that فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is a form of فرط البروتينات الشحمية and is inherited autosomally dominant helps clinicians diagnose, screen families, and manage patients with lipid metabolism abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1608

This category is mapped to type IIa hyperlipidemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ), a condition that follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern (Autosomal dominant inheritance). Recognizing that type IIa hyperlipidemia falls under the broader umbrella of lipid metabolism disorders and is inherited autosomally dominant helps clinicians with diagnosis, genetic counseling, and the management of patients who present with elevated blood lipids.

Hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions are grouped under the congenital, familial, and genetic disorders (الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية, SMQ). The SMQ category includes diffuse encephalitis (الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور), عدم تحمل بروتين اللايسين (lysine intolerance), فالج طفلي (infantile cerebral palsy), متلازمة ستيكلر (Stickle syndrome), as well as broader groups such as genitourinary congenital anomalies, sexually transmitted infections, and other hereditary and degenerative nervous system disorders.
